symbols: bound body tied to a post — captivity and submission to martyrdom for faith
arrows (implied by the binding) — the instruments of Sebastian's execution by Roman archers
swirling dark drapery — the violence and chaos of persecution
upturned face — the martyr's gaze directed heavenward in trust and surrender to God
description: A dramatic charcoal or graphite drawing of a nearly nude young man bound tightly to a wooden post or tree by dark swirling fabric that wraps around his torso and limbs. His head is tilted back and to one side, eyes closed, in an expression of suffering or ecstasy. The figure's body is rendered with fluid, expressive lines. The background is dark and loosely sketched. The overall effect is intensely theatrical and emotionally charged.
theological_description: Saint Sebastian, a Roman soldier secretly Christian, was ordered executed by Emperor Diocletian by being shot with arrows. The early Church saw in Sebastian's martyrdom a mirror of Christ's own Passion: an innocent man bound, pierced, and left for dead by imperial authority. Remarkably, Sebastian survived the arrows and was nursed back to health by Saint Irene, only to confront the emperor again and be beaten to death — making him a martyr twice over. His story became a paradigm of courageous witness: faith that refuses to stay silent even after miraculous deliverance. The bound, pierced body of Sebastian has been read typologically as an image of Christ on the cross (Psalm 22:16, 'they pierced my hands and my feet'). His survival of the first execution prefigures resurrection hope.

scripture_refs.reason: They pierced my hands and feet — the pierced body of the martyr reflecting Christ's own wounds
I have fought the good fight, finished the race — Sebastian's double martyrdom as the ultimate completion of faithful witness
Be faithful unto death and I will give you the crown of life — the promise to martyrs like Sebastian
